aligned_segments.title: The original affluent society
aligned_segments.body:
Number ten. The original affluent society.
We assume early humans spent their days in a relentless grind. Waking before dawn. Scanning the horizon for predators. Spending every waking hour hunting, gathering, fleeing, or recovering from the last hunt. The picture we carry in our heads is a species perpetually on the edge. Every calorie earned through exhaustion. Every night survived by luck. This is the baseline assumption in nearly every textbook, every museum diorama, every documentary that opens with a human silhouette against a savanna sunset. Survival was the full-time job, and there was no time off.
The evidence shows the opposite. In 1966, anthropologist Marshall Sahlins presented data at the Man the Hunter symposium in Chicago that rewrote the narrative. Drawing on fieldwork by Richard Borshay Lee among the !Kung San of the Kalahari Desert, Sahlins demonstrated that hunter-gatherers required roughly 15 to 20 hours of subsistence work per week. A !Kung woman gathered enough food in one day to feed her family for three. The rest of her time was spent resting, visiting neighboring camps, doing embroidery, and entertaining visitors. The men hunted intensely for a few days, then did nothing for weeks.
Fifteen to twenty hours per week. The average American works 38.7 hours. The average medieval European peasant worked closer to 50 during harvest season. The people we draw as primitives had more leisure time than you do right now, sitting wherever you are sitting, watching this video during one of the few free hours in your schedule.
The assumption was never based on evidence. It was based on a story we told ourselves about progress. That civilization rescued us from the wild. Sahlins called it the Zen road to affluence. They did not have more. They wanted less. And that single inversion collapses the foundational myth of every economy built since the Neolithic.
That theory described what they were not doing. This next one is about what they chose to do with all that empty time.

aligned_segments.title: The play hypothesis
aligned_segments.body:
Number nine. The play hypothesis.
We assume that any free time early humans had was spent on practical tasks. Sharpening tools. Reinforcing shelters. Preparing skins. If they were not hunting or gathering, they were maintaining the infrastructure of survival. Leisure, in our model, is a modern invention. A byproduct of surplus. You play when the work is done, and in the Paleolithic, the work was never done.
The archaeological record says early humans played. Not occasionally. Routinely. Knucklebones, the ankle bones of sheep and goats, appear at sites across the Levant and southern Europe dating back over 40,000 years, and the wear patterns on them are consistent with repeated throwing and catching. Carved bone pieces resembling gaming tokens have been recovered from Upper Paleolithic sites in France and Belgium. At Kostenki, a 25,000-year-old settlement along the Don River in Russia, archaeologists found small ivory figurines with no apparent utilitarian function. They were not tools. They were not weapons. They were objects made to be held, examined, and enjoyed.
The oldest known toy-like objects are even older. Rounded stones with carved grooves, found in southern African sites dated to 70,000 years ago, match no known tool typology. They fit in a child's hand.
Seventy thousand years. That is roughly 3,500 generations of humans making things for no survival reason. The time between us and them is deep. But the impulse to sit in a circle and do something pointless together is apparently older than agriculture, older than permanent shelter, and possibly older than spoken language as we know it.
You do the same thing every weekend. You just call it different names.
The same people who carved those objects also did something else with their idle hours. Something even harder to explain.
